one_line_takeaway: With just 100 training pairs and no detector or key access, a capacity-aware RL paraphrasing attack (RLSpoofer) spoofs six LLM watermarking schemes at rates matching or beating baselines trained on 100x more data, most strikingly reaching 62.0% SSR against the distortion-free PF-Watermark versus an 8.75% best baseline.
key_finding: RLSpoofer, trained on only 100 pairs, matches or exceeds baselines trained on up to 10,000/7,000 pairs across all six watermarking schemes and five attacker models. Headline result (Table 1/3): on Qwen3-4B against the distortion-free PF-Watermark, RLSpoofer achieves 62.0% SSR versus the best baseline (DPO) at only 8.75% (Distill 6.0%, DITTO 3.5%). On SWEET with the smallest attacker (Qwen3-0.6B), RLSpoofer reaches 50.5% SSR vs. Distill 20.0% and DITTO 6.75%. Table 2 (EWD, Qwen3-4B) shows Distill and DITTO can reach higher raw Spoof Rate (SR 62.5% and 94.3% respectively) than RLSpoofer's SR of 87.0%, but with lower/similar semantic quality (P-SP 0.81/0.68 vs. RLSpoofer's 0.73). Ablations: the capacity-aware weighting (1 - p_max) beats uniform or reversed (p_max) weighting by large margins (e.g., Qwen3-4B EWD SSR 56.5% vs. 35.5% uniform vs. 28.0% p_max, Table 4); the conservative 'Min.' semantic reward outperforms averaging or one-sided references (Table 5); removing the cross-entropy anchor drops SSR by 23.0-36.5 points, and replacing RL with plain distillation on the same 100 pairs causes near-complete failure (Table 6); RLSpoofer works with as few as 50 training samples (Table 7); the choice of surrogate reference model matters more as attacker capacity grows -- for Qwen3-8B, using a smaller Qwen3-0.6B surrogate instead of self-surrogate raises PF SSR from 29.8% to 58.5% (Figure 3); cross-watermark transfer is strongly asymmetric and directional (e.g., training on PF transfers to EWD at 52.5% SSR but training on PF transfers to SWEET at only 0.25%, Figure 4).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is two-fold: (1) a theoretical 'local capacity bottleneck' (Theorem 1) that bounds, under a fixed KL budget around a human-like next-token distribution, how much probability mass can be reassigned to any token subset while preserving semantic fidelity -- showing the bound tightens to zero as the human-like distribution becomes more concentrated; and (2) RLSpoofer, a GRPO-based reinforcement-learning black-box watermark spoofing attack built on this theory. RLSpoofer trains a paraphrasing policy using only 100 human-watermarked rewrite pairs and zero access to watermark internals, secret keys, or the detector. Its reward combines a capacity-aware token-level term r_t = c_t * log(P_wm(x'_t|h_t)/P_h(x'_t|h_t)) (where c_t = 1 - max_x pi_ref(x|h_t) is the local capacity mass), a sequence-level semantic reward based on the minimum of P-SP similarity to both the original text and the watermarked rewrite, and a cross-entropy anchoring term toward the watermarked rewrite, all approximated using a single lightweight reference model conditioned alternately on the original text (as proxy for P_h) and the watermarked rewrite (as proxy for P_wm).
threat_model: Black-box adversary that wants to spoof a watermark detector into flagging an unwatermarked paraphrase as watermarked while preserving the semantic meaning of a source text. Knowledge: no access to the secret key s, the detector function f, or the threshold delta. Capability: can query a watermarked generator to obtain human-text/watermarked-paraphrase pairs and use them to fine-tune its own paraphrasing model; no detector queries during training or inference. This is an evaluation/attack tool intended to stress-test watermark spoofing resilience, explicitly framed as such by the authors (a 'lightweight evaluator').
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) sampling-based distortion-free watermarks (PF, PMark) remain comparatively more resilient than logit-based ones even to RLSpoofer -- PF SSR (62.0% peak, Qwen3-4B) is markedly lower than logit-based scheme SSRs (often 50-58%) for weaker attackers, and enlarging the PF training set beyond 100 samples does not yield consistent SSR gains (Table 7, PF SSR at 200 samples for Qwen3-4B drops to 25.3% from 60.8% at 100), attributed to PF's distortion-free design creating a harder-to-capture distribution gap. (2) Surrogate-model sensitivity grows with attacker capacity: the strongest attacker (Qwen3-8B) is highly sensitive to which model is used to approximate the target distribution, since its own rewrites too strongly weaken the embedded watermark signal to serve as a good self-surrogate. (3) Cross-watermark transferability is unpredictable/asymmetric rather than governed simply by watermark family (KGW-style EWD/SWEET transfer well to each other, but PF-to-SWEET transfer nearly fails). (4) The distributional surrogate objective and capacity-bottleneck theorem rely on approximating true human-like and watermarked distributions with a single lightweight reference model, an approximation whose fidelity is not independently validated beyond downstream SSR numbers. The paper does not report full detail on generalization to watermarking schemes or model families beyond those tested, nor to non-paraphrase attack surfaces.

quantitative_results: RLSpoofer SSR on PF-Watermark with Qwen3-4B: 62.0% vs. best baseline (DPO) 8.75%
RLSpoofer SSR on SWEET with Qwen3-0.6B: 50.5% vs. Distill 20.0%, DITTO 6.75%, DPO 0.0%
RLSpoofer SSR on EWD with Qwen3-4B: 56.5% vs. Distill 51.3%, DITTO 56.0%, DPO 0.25%
EWD Spoof Rate (SR, Qwen3-4B): RLSpoofer 87.0% vs. Distill 62.5% vs. DITTO 94.3% vs. DPO 0.50%
PF Spoof Rate (SR, Qwen3-4B): RLSpoofer 82.8% vs. Distill 6.50%, DITTO 6.25%, DPO 8.75%
Ablation: capacity weight (1-p_max) vs uniform vs p_max on EWD/Qwen3-4B SSR: 56.5% vs 35.5% vs 28.0%
Ablation: removing cross-entropy anchor drops SSR by 23.0-36.5 points across schemes/models
Ablation: 100-pair supervised distillation (no RL) yields near-zero SSR (e.g., 0.00-0.25%)
Training set size sweep (Qwen3-4B, EWD): SSR 46.5% (50 samples) -> 56.5% (100) -> 58.5% (200)
Surrogate choice sensitivity (Qwen3-8B, PF watermark): SSR 29.8% (self-surrogate) vs. 58.5% (Qwen3-0.6B surrogate)
Cross-watermark transfer asymmetry (Qwen3-4B): PF-trained to EWD-test SSR 52.5% vs PF-trained to SWEET-test SSR 0.25%
baselines_compared: Distill (distills watermarked-model output distribution from 10,000 human-watermarked pairs)
DITTO (spoofing via knowledge distillation, analyzes distilled model's statistical preferences)
